document:
It started because the lawnmower wouldn’t start. Dead battery again. I pulled it halfway out, cables tangled like old spaghetti, and that’s when the avalanche began—paint cans (dried), a speaker with one torn cone, a bag of sand from last winter with a mouse nest inside. The shovel I needed for the path was pinned behind a bike with a flat front tire and a box labeled “Christmas 2017” that hasn’t been opened since.

So now the middle of the floor is a rough map of last decade’s ambitions: camping gear with mildew along the seams, a stand-up paddleboard I used once, three pairs of winter boots that don’t fit. Tomas came by and asked if I was selling anything. I told him no, but he helped shove the old workbench against the wall, which freed up just enough space to roll the mower near the outlet. We left it there, half plugged in.

Found a stack of photo albums too—paper ones, forgotten. One’s labeled “Trip to the coast.” I flipped it open. Me on a beach, Noor laughing, sand in her hair. I set it on the shelf above the freezer, away from damp. Kofi had given me that shelf for my birthday years ago. Holds more than you'd think.

It’s still cluttered. Doesn’t look clean like the ones in magazines. But now there’s a lane. You can walk from the door to the fridge without shifting boxes. The trash bags are in the corner by the bike, filled but not tied, waiting for Thursday. The garden tools are hanging now, uneven but up. A coil of hose dangles from a nail, not perfect, but off the floor.

I wiped the dust off the light fixture. Brighter. Not good yet. But different.

It started with the bike. Not even mine—Ingrid’s old hybrid, the one with the punctured rear tyre and the frayed brake cable. It was leaning against the freezer, like it had given up mid-escape. I’d been meaning to fix it for a month, maybe two. Just needed a patch, a bit of oil. But every time I thought about it, my mind drifted to the other things in the way: the stack of flattened boxes that never made it to recycling, the toolbox with the cracked hinge spilling screwdrivers, the Christmas lights tangled like last year’s guilt.

Today, though, something shifted. Maybe it was the smell—the damp concrete, the faint mildew from the camping tent left rolled too tight for too long. Maybe it was the way the single bulb flickered when I tugged the cord. I opened the big door, let in the grey afternoon, and stood there with my arms crossed, just taking it in. Not judging, exactly. More like acknowledging.

I dragged the bike outside. Then the boxes. Then the tent, unrolled it on the driveway even though the grass was wet. Found a dead wasp inside the vestibule. Set it aside. Moved the freezer an inch to the left so I could finally reach the mop bucket behind it. The bucket was full of random things—half-used paint can, Kofi’s old skateboard (why is it here?), a frayed rope.

Hours passed without me noticing. No system, no grand plan. Just one thing after another finding a place, or being set aside for later, or thrown straight into the bin without ceremony. At one point Sanjay called from the house, asking if I wanted coffee. I said yes, then forgot to come in, forgot the cup sat cooling on the windowsill.

Now it’s not tidy, exactly. Still lopsided. The shelves sag, and there’s a pile near the back I’ll deal with tomorrow, or not. But the bike’s in the corner, ready. The door can close. The air feels different. I stood there again just now, hands on hips, looking. Not finished. But changed. The light’s low, and the dust is still settling.

The footpaths through the western edge of the neighbourhood follow no formal design. They aren’t paved, only compacted by use over time, and they diverge from the grid of official sidewalks like tributaries. You won’t find them on most maps—certainly not the ones the city distributes with bus routes and zoning boundaries. They’re narrow, mostly half a metre across, worn into the soft soil between houses, under low branches, around patches of brambles that bloom late in the season.

I walk one of them most mornings. It cuts behind the row of converted duplexes near the schoolyard and leads into the strip of unmanaged land that runs parallel to the service road. The path banks slightly at the midpoint, where water collects during heavy rain. In spring, you can still see the faint channel from last melt. In summer, it’s dust and the odd cracked acorn husk underfoot. The vegetation closes in from either side—birch saplings, mostly, and wild grasses that bend over the track like a tunnel.

This one path shows signs of multiple origins. Near the start, where it passes behind Amara’s shed, the edges are trampled wide. She cycles through often with the dog, and others follow. There’s a worn patch on the left where her bike wheel always tracks. Further on, the route narrows again. Hana uses it in the afternoons on her way home from the corner store, and her gait is deliberate, her steps small. You can see it in the compression of the soil—less force, but more repetitions. The pattern of wear isn’t consistent the way a machine would lay a line; it’s hesitant, adjusting. It reflects mood, weather, which shoe was worn that day.

Yuki says the routes were already there when her family moved in twenty years ago. She remembers seeing an older man walking it every dawn, always in a grey coat, long before the new building went up across the ravine. The path didn’t lead anywhere then—just ended at a fence. Still, he walked it, round and back. Now that fence is gone, and more people use the route, but the path hasn’t widened much. It has a kind of inertia.

There’s something about the way these paths persist—not from planning, but from the accumulation of small, unremarked choices. They map intention without declaration. No signage, no permissions sought. Only the slow agreement between boots and soil. This particular one shifts slightly each season. Last autumn, a fallen limb redirected the flow for weeks, and the new curve remained, just barely, after it was cleared. Another imperfection accepted.

This morning, frost hardened the upper layer, and the track held the shape of the last passerby—a shallow impression, heel-first. I stepped into it, not deliberately, and continued on.

I’ve made coffee the same way in the morning for years—same pot, same grounds, same ritual—until last winter when the old electric stopped working. Something in the pump gave out after a dry click. I don’t know how to fix things, so I did what I could: I made coffee over the stove in a saucepan, like my mother used to when I was a kid. She called it cowboy coffee. I added the grounds to cold water, brought it to a simmer, counted to thirty, then lifted it off. The texture was murky, the surface littered with sediment, and still, there was something comforting in the improvisation. I drank it from a chipped mug, standing by the window while watching the day fail to start properly outside: gray sky, gray trees, a single squirrel blinking on the fence post.

When the kettle’s cord burned through at work, someone brought in a French press. I borrowed it for a few days, rinsed it out before first use, stirred the grounds slowly as though timing a prayer. The coffee was dark and insistent. It refused to let the water be water. For an afternoon, I felt clear-headed, jumpy. Ingrid made a face the first time she tried it from my cup—too bitter, she said—and I understood. It was too much if you weren’t expecting it. But I kept using it, just to feel the weight of the glass in my hands, to see how light bent through the liquid in mid-morning.

Then Luca came over one Saturday and brought a hand grinder. He didn’t say much, just started adjusting the burrs while I poured water into the gooseneck kettle. He measured beans like he was weighing salt. I watched the steam rise as he poured in slow spirals. The smell was different—brighter, floral. I don’t know what beans he used. I didn’t ask. While the coffee dripped through, he told me about waking before dawn to hike uphill with only a thermos and a flashlight. I didn’t know people still did that. When I tasted it, I almost said something about the clarity again, but I didn’t want to repeat myself.

Now I switch between methods depending on how tired I am, or whether I remembered to buy filters. The stove, the press, the pour-over—each feels like a different friend. One loud, one quiet, one particular. I keep the French press on the counter even though it’s mostly clean. It catches the evening light. When Devon saw it, he asked if I’d gone “fancy.” I didn’t answer.  

The glass is still full this morning, catching dust.

It’s been three weekends now, lifting, stacking, shifting, and still there’s only a narrow path through the middle of the garage. Not enough for the lawnmower to get through—had to leave it on the driveway overnight last Tuesday. Rain came down around midnight. The blades rusted before first use this spring. Now it’s just another thing to deal with.

Most of what’s piled up has no immediate use. That’s obvious. But the trouble isn’t recognising use—it’s assigning weight to it. A broken hedge trimmer from five years ago? The box of ceiling tiles replaced in the remodel? A child’s balance bike from long before they learned to ride? All of it has a history. Pull one thing out and you're suddenly explaining to someone why there’s a single roller skate in the back left corner.

Noor helped the first day. She carried out the garden hoses—kinked and split at the connectors—and set them in a loop by the curb. Later, Ruth came out, took one look at the bin I’d marked “Charity” and asked if I’d seen her folding table. I hadn’t, but then found it behind the old shelving unit, buried under a tarp that had taken on the texture of wet cardboard. We stood it upright. She wiped her hands on her jeans and said, “That’s two things accounted for. Not bad.” Then she went back inside.

The shelving unit itself is probably staying. Sturdy metal, not ideal, but functional. It leans a bit to the right from a bent bracket. Weights on the lower shelves keep it upright. Better than replacing it—unless it finally gives. Then what? Another purchase. Another decision.

There are two boxes I haven’t opened. One is labelled “Xmas,” though I think it’s mostly old electronics. The other says “Tools?” with a question mark. Inside might be a drill bit set, maybe an extender cord. Or something that once belonged to someone else. Hard to say without opening it, and opening leads to sorting, and sorting requires a decision: keep, discard, donate.

Priya walked through this morning, glanced at the progress. “Looks better,” she said. There was a pause. “But kind of the same, somehow.” She wasn’t wrong.

The bin bags are mostly full of insulation scraps and packaging. Heavy, but destined for disposal. I’ve scheduled pickup. Beyond that, it’s not clear what counts as finished. At some point, you just stop. The space doesn’t need to make sense. It just needs to
